school: Umayyad general — crossed the Strait of Gibraltar in 711 with 7,000 men; conquered the Visigoth kingdom; Gibraltar is named after him (Jabal al-Tariq)
quotes: The sea is behind you and the enemy is before you — where will you flee?
Know that I am the first to do what I command you to do and the first to forbid what I forbid myself.
Victory comes not from numbers but from certainty.

school: Umayyad general — conquered Sind (modern Pakistan) at age 17; opened South Asia to Islam; one of the most remarkable military careers in history
quotes: Justice is the first weapon of the conqueror — without it, he has only taken land, not hearts.
The people must feel safe — an army that terrorises its own subjects has already lost.
Administer what God has given you as a trust, not as a possession.

school: Zayd ibn Ali ibn Husayn (695–740), great-grandson of the Prophet and founder of the Zaidi school of Shia Islam. He led a revolt against the Umayyads and was killed in battle. The Zaidi tradition became the state religion of Yemen for over a thousand years.

Abu Hanifa al-Nu'man ibn Thabit (699–767), founder of the Hanafi school, the largest school of Islamic jurisprudence followed by approximately 45% of the world's Muslims. A merchant by trade and a tabi'i, he pioneered the use of analogical reasoning (qiyas) and juristic preference (istihsan) in Islamic law.
quotes: Opinion is the use of reason in the absence of divine text.
The opinion is only my opinion — if someone brings me something better, I will take it.
Whoever seeks knowledge for the sake of the Hereafter gains much; whoever seeks it for the sake of the world gains little.
The greatest jurisprudence is the knowledge of the self — what serves it and what harms it.
Whoever uses analogy without the Sunnah will go astray.
The scholar must fear God in his knowledge just as the ruler must fear God in his power.

school: Founder of the Syrian (Awza'i) school of jurisprudence, which dominated the Islamic west for two centuries before being superseded by Malikism; mufti of the military frontier towns; famously debated with Abu Yusuf (Hanafi) over legal methodology; his school was influential in Andalus; died in Beirut which has a district named after him
quotes: Hold fast to the traces of those who came before you, even if the people reject you.
The one who follows the Sunnah is never truly alone — even if he stands by himself.
Follow the traces of those who have gone before you, even if the people abandon you.
No people have abandoned a Sunnah except that God afflicted them with what is worse.
The sign of a scholar is that he speaks little and listens much.

school: Malik ibn Anas (711–795), founder of the Maliki school of jurisprudence, spent his entire life in Madinah where he compiled the Muwatta, the earliest surviving collection of hadith and legal rulings. He incorporated the living practice of Madinah as a source of law.

books.title: Al-Muwatta (The Well-Trodden Path)
books.note: Oldest surviving complete book of Islamic law and hadith — predates al-Bukhari.

parents: Anas bin Abi Aans (Malik) / Aaliyah bint Shurayk al-Azdiyya
quotes: The last of this community will not be reformed except by what reformed its first.
The Sunnah is like Noah's Ark — whoever boards it is saved, and whoever stays behind is drowned.
Do not say of any matter, 'I will do this', unless you also say, 'if Allah wills'.
The last of this community will not be rectified except by what rectified its first.
None of you should say: 'My opinion is correct but possibly wrong.' Say instead: 'This is what I believe, and God knows best.'
Knowledge is a light that God places wherever He wills — it is not merely the quantity of narrations.

school: The leading scholar of Egypt and founder of an independent legal school; al-Shafi'i said: 'al-Layth is more learned in jurisprudence than Malik, but his followers were negligent in transmitting his opinions'; known for extraordinary generosity — he reportedly spent 120,000 dinars in charity in a single year; his school was eventually absorbed by Malikism in Egypt
quotes: Malik’s rulings are sound for the people of Medina — but they do not always suit the people of Egypt.
The scholar who does not adapt his knowledge to his context has not yet mastered it.
Jurisprudence is not one size — it fits the people it serves.
Malik’s rulings are sound for the people of Medina — but they do not always suit the people of Egypt.
The scholar who does not adapt his knowledge to his context has not yet mastered it.
Jurisprudence is not one size — it fits the people it serves.

school: Called 'the Commander of the Faithful in Hadith' for his unparalleled mastery of hadith criticism; founder of an independent legal school (later absorbed into Hanbalism); fled authorities to avoid being forced to serve; a fierce critic of Umayyad and Abbasid rulers; his asceticism and scholarship are legendary — 'Whoever Allah wishes good for, He makes him like Sufyan al-Thawri'
parents: Sa'id bin Masrooq Ath-Thawri / A pious woman
quotes: We sought knowledge for reasons other than God — but knowledge refused to be sought for anything less.
If you see a scholar who loves the world, do not trust him with your religion.

school: An eighth-century Persian scholar and translator who played a pivotal role in transmitting Persian and Indian literary traditions into Arabic. His translation of the Panchatantra into Arabic as Kalila wa-Dimna became one of the most widely read works of Arabic prose literature. He also translated important Sasanian works on statecraft and governance. He was executed around 756 CE, reportedly on charges of heresy, though political motivations are widely suspected.
quotes: The intelligent man learns from the experience of others — the fool learns only from his own.
A king who has no wise counsellor is like an eye without sight.
Speak the truth to the powerful even when they hate it — silence in that moment is betrayal.

school: Primary student of Imam Abu Hanifa who codified Hanafi administrative and constitutional law; appointed first Chief Judge (Qadi al-Quzat) of the Abbasid Caliphate by Harun al-Rashid; his Kitab al-Kharaj is the foundational text of Islamic fiscal and land law
quotes: The ruler has no right to the people’s wealth except what the law specifies — and the law is God’s, not the ruler’s.
Taxation without justice is confiscation.
The judge who fears the powerful and pities the weak has understood nothing about justice.

school: The most authoritative student of Imam Malik; his Al-Mudawwana (compiled by Sahnun from his transmissions) became the foundational text of Maliki law; spent 20 years in Imam Malik's circle and transmitted the vast majority of the Muwatta rulings that formed the Maliki school in Egypt and North Africa
parents: al-Qasim ibn Muhammad / Fatima bint al-Mundhir bin al-Zubayr

school: Student of both Abu Hanifa and Imam Malik; systematised the Hanafi legal corpus into the authoritative Zahir al-Riwaya (Six Books); his Siyar al-Kabir is the earliest and most comprehensive treatise on Islamic international law governing relations between Muslim and non-Muslim states
quotes: International relations between nations must be governed by principles — not merely by power.
The law of nations is not invented by rulers — it is discovered by scholars.
Even in war there are rules — and whoever abandons them has abandoned civilisation.

school: Muhammad ibn Idris al-Shafi'i (767–820), founder of the Shafi'i school of jurisprudence and the first to systematise Islamic legal theory in his Risala. He synthesised the approaches of the Hanafi rationalists and the Maliki traditionalists, establishing the Quran, Sunnah, consensus, and analogy as the four sources of law.
quotes: Knowledge is not what is memorised. Knowledge is what benefits.
Knowledge is not what is memorised — knowledge is what benefits.
Whoever wishes to know the world should study history; whoever wishes to know himself should study jurisprudence.
Whoever wishes that God open the light of his heart must leave what does not concern him, abandon sin, and shun disputes.
The more I learn, the more I discover how much I do not know.
If you find that my opinion contradicts the hadith of the Prophet, follow the hadith and throw my opinion against the wall.

school: Abu Uthman Amr ibn Bahr al-Jahiz (776–868) was the towering polymath of Abbasid-era Arabic letters — essayist, zoologist, satirist, and theologian — widely regarded as the father of Arabic prose literature. Born in Basra to a family of modest means (possibly of East African descent), he studied under the greatest scholars of Basra and Baghdad and became the leading intellectual of the Mu'tazili school. His Kitab al-Hayawan (Book of Animals) is a seven-volume masterwork blending zoology with theology, philosophy, and Arabic literary culture. His Kitab al-Bukhala (Book of Misers) remains a classic of Arabic comic prose. Al-Jahiz wrote over 200 works on topics from rhetoric and politics to theology and natural science. His vivid, witty, digressive prose style permanently shaped the Arabic essay tradition.
quotes: Whoever knows himself knows his Lord.

school: Compiled Al-Mudawwana al-Kubra from Ibn al-Qasim's transmissions of Imam Malik — the text that defined Maliki jurisprudence for North Africa, Spain, and West Africa for a millennium; served as Chief Judge (Qadi) of Kairouan and established it as a centre of Maliki learning, directly influencing the Timbuktu tradition
Hanbali School — Founder. Dominant in Arabian Peninsula. Symbol of Sunni orthodoxy against state theology.
parents: Muhammad Ibn Hanbal / Saffiyah bint Maimoonah bint 'Abdul Malik
quotes: Patience is to faith what the head is to the body.
If you see a man who loves to debate and argue, avoid him — for he has been afflicted.
The Sunnah in my view is to hold fast to what the companions of the Messenger were upon.
If you want God to be with you wherever you are, be with God wherever He commands.
The foundation of the Sunnah in our view is holding fast to what the Companions of the Messenger of God were upon.
Whoever is patient with the difficulties of this life will inherit the ease of the next.

school: Shafi'i School — al-Shafi'i's most distinguished student; his Mukhtasar is the foundational Shafi'i legal text

A ninth-century Persian geographer and bureaucrat who served as the Abbasid postmaster of Jibal province, he authored Kitab al-Masalik wa-l-Mamalik (Book of Roads and Kingdoms), one of the earliest surviving Arabic geographical works. The book catalogued the postal routes, provinces, and tax revenues of the Abbasid caliphate, serving as both a geographical reference and an administrative handbook. His work also contains some of the earliest Arabic references to China, Korea, and Japan.

A ninth-century Persian historian who lived in Baghdad, he authored two major historical works: Futuh al-Buldan (Conquests of the Lands) and Ansab al-Ashraf (Genealogies of the Nobles). His Futuh al-Buldan remains one of the most important primary sources for the early Muslim conquests and the administrative organisation of the early caliphate. He served at the Abbasid court and was known for his meticulous approach to historical documentation.
quotes: History is the record of what people leave behind, not what they carry with them.

school: Baghdadi / Independent Jariri school — Founded the science of historical narrative (isnad-based history)
quotes: Every day of your life is a page of your history.
I have compiled this history so that those who come after may learn from those who came before.
Knowledge is a trust — the one who carries it must carry it faithfully.
The one who learns history learns the fate of nations — and the one who ignores it shares their fate.
I have collected in this book what the scholars have said — and I leave the judgment to the reader.

school: Hanafi School — switched from Shafi'i to Hanafi under his maternal uncle Al-Muzani; his Al-Aqida al-Tahawiyya is the most universally accepted creedal text in Sunni Islam

A ninth-century historian and geographer of Abbasid Baghdad, he authored Tarikh al-Yaqubi (History of al-Yaqubi), a universal history from creation to his own time, and Kitab al-Buldan (Book of Countries). His historical work is notable for its attention to non-Arab and pre-Islamic civilisations, reflecting his broad intellectual curiosity. He travelled widely across the Islamic world and his geographical writings draw on firsthand observation.

A tenth-century Persian geographer who authored Kitab al-Masalik wa-l-Mamalik (Book of Roads and Kingdoms), one of the foundational works of the Balkhi school of Islamic geography. His work built upon the earlier geographical tradition of al-Balkhi and included detailed maps of the Islamic world's provinces. His systematic approach to regional description influenced subsequent geographers including Ibn Hawqal.
Maturidi School of Kalam — Founder. Paired with Ash'ari as the two pillars of Sunni theology. Dominant among Hanafi Muslims.
quotes: Reason and revelation are complementary — neither contradicts the other when properly understood.

school: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari (874–936), founder of the Ash'ari school of theology, the dominant theological tradition in Sunni Islam. Originally a Mu'tazili rationalist, he famously broke with his teachers and developed a middle path using rational argument to defend orthodox beliefs.
quotes: We affirm what God has affirmed for Himself, and deny what He has denied, without asking how.
I hold what the pious ancestors held — but I defend it with the tools of reason.
The Quran is the speech of God — uncreated in its essence, carried in created letters.
Between pure rationalism and pure literalism lies the straight path.

school: A tenth-century Abbasid-era scholar, chess master, and courtier, he was renowned as one of the greatest chess players of the medieval Islamic world. He served as tutor and companion to several Abbasid caliphs and compiled important anthologies of Arabic poetry. His works on the reigns of the caliphs he served provide valuable primary source material for Abbasid court history.

A tenth-century Yemeni polymath, he excelled in geography, history, astronomy, and poetry. His major work Sifat Jazirat al-Arab (Description of the Arabian Peninsula) is the most comprehensive medieval geographical account of Arabia, and his Iklil (The Crown) preserves the genealogies and history of South Arabian peoples. He is considered one of the foremost scholars of Yemeni heritage and Arabian geography.

Abu al-Hasan al-Mas'udi (896–956), the 'Herodotus of the Arabs,' was the first historian to combine geography, natural history, and world history into a single narrative. His Muruj al-Dhahab covers civilisations from China to the Franks.
quotes: The wise man is he who knows himself.

school: A tenth-century Arab scholar and litterateur based in Baghdad, he is best known for his monumental work Kitab al-Aghani (Book of Songs), a multi-volume encyclopaedia of Arabic poetry, music, and literary history. The work preserves invaluable information about pre-Islamic and early Islamic culture, poets, and musicians that would otherwise have been lost. He spent over fifty years compiling the work, drawing on both written sources and oral traditions.
quotes: Poetry is the archive of the Arabs — through it their wisdom, their wars, and their loves are preserved.
The singer gives the poem a second life — music is the soul that text alone cannot carry.
I collected what would have been lost — that is a scholar’s most urgent duty.

school: A tenth-century Baghdadi bibliographer and bookseller, he compiled al-Fihrist (The Catalogue), an extraordinary index of all books written in Arabic up to his time. The work catalogues thousands of titles across every field of knowledge, from theology and philosophy to natural sciences and literature, providing an unparalleled snapshot of Islamic intellectual life. His Fihrist remains an indispensable reference for understanding the scope and breadth of medieval Arabic scholarship.

Abu al-Hasan al-Qabisi (936–1012), the leading Maliki jurist and educator of Qayrawan, Tunisia. His al-Risala al-Mufassala is the earliest surviving Islamic treatise devoted entirely to educational theory.
quotes: The child who is not taught the Quran has been deprived of the greatest gift.
Education is not the filling of a vessel — it is the lighting of a fire.
The teacher’s responsibility is the heaviest in any society — for he shapes what all others will become.

school: A tenth-century Arab geographer and traveller, he authored Surat al-Ard (Face of the Earth), a comprehensive geographical account of the Islamic world based on decades of personal travel. He revised and expanded the earlier work of al-Istakhri, adding detailed observations of trade routes, economies, and populations. His accounts of Sicily, North Africa, and the Saharan trade routes are among the most detailed surviving from the medieval period.

A tenth-century Palestinian geographer who authored Ahsan al-Taqasim fi Ma'rifat al-Aqalim (The Best Divisions for Knowledge of the Regions), widely regarded as the finest geographical work of the medieval Islamic world. Unlike many predecessors, he travelled extensively throughout the Islamic lands he described, bringing personal observation and critical analysis to his accounts. His work combined physical geography with detailed information about local economies, customs, and dialects.
quotes: I have visited every region of the earth, and I have found no people more generous than the people of the Levant.

school: Shaykh al-Mufid (c. 948–1022), the most important Twelver Shia theologian of the Buyid period in Baghdad. He systematised Shia doctrine, trained the next generation of scholars including al-Sharif al-Radi, and defended Shia positions against both Sunni and Mu’tazili critics. His Kitab al-Irshad is a foundational Shia biographical text.

Abu Bakr al-Baqillani (d. 1013) was the foremost Ash'ari theologian and legal theorist of his generation, serving as the chief systematiser of Ash'ari kalam after al-Ash'ari himself. Born in Basra and educated in Baghdad, he became a qadi and was sent as a diplomatic envoy to the Byzantine court, where he reportedly impressed the emperor with his debating skill. His most celebrated work, Kitab al-Tamhid, laid the foundations of Sunni rational theology by refining the arguments for God's existence, prophethood, and the inimitability of the Quran (i'jaz al-Quran). His treatise I'jaz al-Quran remains a landmark in Arabic literary theory. Al-Baqillani trained a generation of Ash'ari scholars and was praised by Ibn Khaldun as the most brilliant mutakallim of his era.
quotes: The rational mind is the first obligation — for without it, no religious duty can be fulfilled.

school: A tenth-century Persian-born Arabic prose writer, he is credited with inventing the maqama literary genre, a form of rhetorical fiction combining narrative prose with poetry. His Maqamat al-Hamadhani featured the adventures of a witty rogue named Abu al-Fath al-Iskandari, told through an unreliable narrator. This literary innovation profoundly influenced Arabic literature and inspired al-Hariri's celebrated maqamat a century later.

An eleventh-century Shafi'i jurist and political theorist based in Baghdad, he authored al-Ahkam al-Sultaniyyah (The Ordinances of Government), one of the most influential treatises on Islamic constitutional law and political theory. The work systematically defined the qualifications, duties, and limits of the caliph's authority, as well as the roles of judges, governors, and other officials. He served as a chief judge and diplomatic envoy for the Abbasid caliphs and his legal framework shaped Sunni political thought for centuries.
quotes: Justice is the foundation of sovereignty.
Leadership is established to succeed the prophethood in the protection of the faith and the management of worldly affairs.
Justice is the foundation of sovereignty — the ruler who abandons it has abandoned the purpose of his rule.
Leadership is established to protect the faith and manage worldly affairs.
Justice is the foundation of sovereignty — the ruler who abandons it has abandoned his purpose.

books.title: Al-Ahkam al-Sultaniyyah (The Laws of Islamic Governance)

school: Abu Muhammad Ali ibn Hazm (994–1064), the leading figure of the Zahiri school. His Tawq al-Hamama (Ring of the Dove) is a classic treatise on love, while his al-Fisal is the first systematic comparative study of world religions.

books.title: The Ring of the Dove (Tawq al-Hamama)

quotes: Every soul pleased with itself is ignoble; every soul displeased with itself is noble.
Whoever points out your faults to you has given you a great gift.
Time is not gold — time is life itself.

school: Shams al-A'imma al-Sarakhsi (1009–1090) was the greatest Hanafi jurist of the medieval period, known by the honorific 'Sun of the Imams.' Born in Sarakhs (in present-day Turkmenistan), he studied under the leading Hanafi scholars of Central Asia. His masterwork, al-Mabsut, is a thirty-volume encyclopedia of Hanafi jurisprudence that he reportedly dictated from memory while imprisoned in a well (or underground cell) by a local ruler for a legal opinion that displeased the court. The al-Mabsut remains the most authoritative reference in Hanafi fiqh. He also wrote Usul al-Sarakhsi on legal methodology, which became a standard text in Hanafi usul al-fiqh. His ability to produce such vast works from prison, without access to a library, became legendary.
quotes: The foundations of jurisprudence are four: the Book, the Sunnah, consensus, and analogy — and all four are one light.
A ruling built on hardship is not a ruling — it is a burden, and burdens are not the way of this religion.

school: An eleventh-century Andalusian geographer and historian, he authored Kitab al-Masalik wa-l-Mamalik (Book of Roads and Kingdoms), one of the most important geographical works of medieval Islam. Notably, he never travelled outside the Iberian Peninsula, composing his geographical accounts entirely from literary sources and travellers' reports. His works provide some of the earliest detailed descriptions of West Africa and the Ghana Empire.
Abu al-Ma'ali al-Juwayni (1028–1085), known as 'Imam al-Haramayn,' the greatest Ash'ari theologian before al-Ghazali and al-Ghazali's primary teacher at the Nizamiyya madrasa in Nishapur.
quotes: The purpose of theology is to know God as He truly is.
Knowledge is not the abundance of narrations — knowledge is a light that God places in the heart.
The one who masters the foundations needs no authority but his own understanding.
The scholar who has not mastered disagreement has not mastered agreement — for he does not know what he holds or why.
Certainty is reached by evidence — whoever claims certainty without evidence has only claimed it.
The purpose of theology is not victory in debate — it is the protection of faith.

school: Turkic lexicographer and scholar (1008–1105) who compiled the Diwan Lughat al-Turk, the first comprehensive dictionary of Turkic languages, presented to the Abbasid caliph in Baghdad. The work is an invaluable record of Turkic culture, geography, folklore, and language at a time when these were rarely documented in writing.
Shafi'i / Khorasan School — leading Shafi'i jurist; his Tafsir is an authoritative Quranic commentary
Almohad movement founder — studied in Baghdad; returned to Morocco and founded the Almohad dynasty based on strict tawhid

Qadi Iyad ibn Musa al-Yahsubi (1083–1149), the most celebrated Maliki scholar of medieval al-Andalus and the Maghreb. His al-Shifa bi-Ta'rif Huquq al-Mustafa became the most widely read work on the Prophet's character.
quotes: Love of the Prophet is an obligation — whoever loves him will strive to follow his example in all things.
The greatest honour bestowed upon this nation is the character of its Prophet.

school: Islamic Geography — served the Norman King Roger II of Sicily, producing the Tabula Rogeriana, the most accurate world map of the medieval era; it remained unsurpassed for three centuries
quotes: The earth is round like a sphere, and the waters cling to it naturally.
I have verified all I have written through my own travels and through the testimonies of those whose reliability I have confirmed.
The world is round as a sphere — and its waters are held in place by natural balance.
Geography is not merely the description of the earth — it is the mirror in which civilisation sees itself.
A people who do not know their land do not know themselves.

school: Shafi'i / Syrian Hadith School — his Tarikh Dimashq is the most comprehensive Islamic biographical dictionary
quotes: The history of Damascus is the history of Islam — for this city has been a beacon of learning.
The scholar’s duty is to preserve the truth — whether the powerful welcome it or not.

school: Abu al-Faraj Ibn al-Jawzi (1116–1201), the greatest Hanbali preacher of medieval Baghdad whose sermons reportedly drew crowds of over 100,000. He wrote over 700 works spanning tafsir, hadith, history, and biography.
quotes: Time is your capital — do not spend it except on what will last.
The intelligent person does not waste a single moment — for moments are the capital of his life.
Lower your eyes from what is forbidden and your heart will be illuminated.

school: Ayyubid Sultan — recaptured Jerusalem in 1187 ending 88 years of Crusader rule; known for chivalry even to his enemies; Richard I of England called him 'the greatest king in the world'
quotes: Beware of bloodshed — never trust in spilled blood, for spilled blood never sleeps.
The strength of a ruler lies not in conquering enemies, but in conquering his own desires.
I warn you against shedding blood without justice — blood that is shed never sleeps.
It is not the walls of Jerusalem that make it holy — it is the hearts of those who love it.
The greatest victory I seek is not conquest of lands but conquest of my own self.

school: Maliki school — his travel account of the Hajj and Islamic world is the first great Islamic travel narrative
quotes: Travel is a foretaste of paradise for those with pure hearts.

school: Hanbali school — the greatest medieval Hanbali jurist; his al-Mughni is the definitive Hanbali legal reference
quotes: Whoever occupies himself with what does not concern him misses what does concern him.

school: Ash'ari / Avicennian Theology — greatest medieval Islamic encyclopaedist; debated across Central Asia
quotes: The greater the difficulty, the more glory in surmounting it.
The end result of human intellect is bewilderment — and the end result of bewilderment is silence.
A scholar who abandons his knowledge for the sake of worldly reward has sold his soul at the cheapest price.

school: A thirteenth-century Kurdish historian from Mosul, he authored al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh (The Complete History), one of the most comprehensive universal chronicles in Arabic historiography. The work covers history from the creation to 1231 CE and is particularly valued for its detailed accounts of the Crusades and the Mongol invasions from a Muslim perspective. He also compiled Usd al-Ghaba, a major biographical dictionary of the Companions of the Prophet.
quotes: History does not repeat itself — but men repeat themselves, and so history appears to repeat.
The chronicler who flatters the powerful has betrayed his craft.

school: A thirteenth-century geographer of Greek origin who was enslaved as a child and later freed, he compiled Mu'jam al-Buldan (Dictionary of Countries), the most comprehensive geographical encyclopaedia of the medieval Islamic world. The work contains detailed entries on thousands of places, combining geographical data with historical, literary, and biographical information. He completed much of his writing while fleeing the Mongol invasions, preserving knowledge of cities and libraries that were subsequently destroyed.
quotes: The earth is vast, and every corner of it contains a lesson for those who travel with open eyes.
The city is not its walls — it is its scholars, its markets, and its memory.
I was a slave who was freed by learning — for knowledge makes every man free.

school: Ilkhanid Court — most widely read Islamic cosmographer; his encyclopedia was copied and illustrated for centuries
Akbarian — stepson and greatest student of Ibn Arabi; systematised Akbarian metaphysics; corresponded with Nasir al-Din al-Tusi
quotes: The master who cannot see God in everything has not yet begun to see God.
The path of knowledge and the path of love are not two paths — they are one path seen from two angles.

school: Jamal al-Din Ibn Wasil (1208–1298), Ayyubid-era historian whose Mufarrij al-Kurub is the most important Arabic primary source for the Crusader period from the Muslim perspective.
Shafi'i School — Chief Judge of Syria; his biographical dictionary set the standard for Islamic biography
quotes: Biography is the noblest branch of history — for through the lives of great men we learn the meaning of greatness.
Every life contains a lesson — and the biographer’s task is to find it.

school: Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Qurtubi (1214–1273) was the greatest Quranic commentator of Islamic Spain. Born in Cordoba during the final years of Muslim Andalusia, he emigrated to Egypt after the Christian reconquest of his homeland. His magnum opus, al-Jami' li-Ahkam al-Quran (The Compendium of Quranic Rulings), is one of the most comprehensive and widely used Quran commentaries in Sunni Islam. Unlike purely theological or mystical tafsirs, al-Qurtubi's work systematically extracts legal rulings from each verse while engaging with grammar, variant readings, and the opinions of earlier scholars. He was a Maliki jurist who also wrote on eschatology (al-Tadhkira) and the afterlife. He spent his final years in Upper Egypt, where he is buried.
quotes: The Quran was not revealed to be placed on shelves — it was revealed to be acted upon.
The scholar who acts on his knowledge is like the sun — it gives light to others while burning itself.

school: Shadhili Sufi master and scholar (d. 1309) based in Cairo who wrote the Kitab al-Hikam (Book of Aphorisms), one of the most widely read texts in the entire Sufi tradition. His pithy, paradoxical wisdom sayings are memorised across the Muslim world. He also wrote the first biography of the Shadhili order’s founders.
quotes: Do not seek His company through your own company with yourself — seek it by your fleeing from yourself to Him.

school: Hasan ibn Yusuf ibn al-Mutahhar al-Hilli (1250–1325), the most prolific and influential Twelver Shia scholar of the medieval period. He wrote over 500 works on jurisprudence, theology, and philosophy. He secured the conversion of the Mongol Ilkhan Oljaitu to Shia Islam.

Ottoman scholar (d. 1350) who was the first Shaykh al-Islam appointed by Sultan Orhan, making him the highest religious authority of the early Ottoman state. He was a master of Ibn Arabi’s philosophy and wrote key commentaries on the Fusus al-Hikam, thereby introducing Akbarian thought into the Ottoman intellectual tradition.
Hanbali / Reformist — Condemned many Sufi practices; most influential and most controversial Islamic reformer of the medieval period

books.title: Al-Uboodiyah (Being a True Slave of Allah)

quotes: The heart will not find complete happiness except by loving God and by striving towards what is dear to Him.
The heart will not find complete happiness except by loving Allah and by striving towards what is dear to Him.
Patience is half of faith, and certainty is faith in its entirety.
The greatest punishment is to be veiled from God and to have a hardened heart.
Whoever truly knows God finds that all matters become easy.
The measure of the heart’s life is the measure of its connection to God.

school: Firdawsiyya — greatest Sufi of medieval Bihar; his letters on the spiritual path are classics of Indo-Persian literature

Adud al-Din al-Iji (1281–1355) was one of the most influential Ash'ari theologians and jurists of the post-classical period. Born in the town of Ij near Shiraz, he rose to become chief judge of the Muzaffarid domains in southern Iran. His masterwork al-Mawaqif fi 'Ilm al-Kalam (Stations in the Science of Theology) became the most widely studied advanced text in Sunni systematic theology for over four centuries, generating dozens of commentaries — most notably by al-Jurjani. He also wrote on jurisprudential theory (Sharh Mukhtasar Ibn al-Hajib) and rhetoric. Al-Iji represents the mature synthesis of Ash'ari theology with Avicennan philosophy that defined late Sunni intellectual culture. He died in prison after falling out of favour with the Muzaffarid ruler.

Shams al-Din Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya (1292–1350), the most distinguished student of Ibn Taymiyyah. His works uniquely combine rigorous legal scholarship with profound spiritual psychology — his Madarij al-Salikin maps the stations of the spiritual path.

books.title: Al-Fawaid: A Collection of Wise Sayings

quotes: The heart is like a mirror. If you do not keep polishing it, the rust will cover it.
The heart becomes sick just as the body becomes sick — and its medicine is tawbah and protection from harm.
Patience is of three types: patience in obeying God, patience in refraining from disobedience, and patience with God's decrees.
The heart will rest and feel relief if it is settled with God.
Your time is your life — and your life is your capital.
Sins are like chains — every sin you commit adds a link.

school: A fourteenth-century Mamluk-era scholar and biographer, he compiled al-Wafi bi-l-Wafayat (The Complete in Recording Deaths), one of the largest biographical dictionaries in Arabic literature, containing over fourteen thousand entries. He served in various administrative positions across Syria and Egypt while maintaining a prodigious scholarly output. His biographical method combined historical rigour with literary flair, preserving information about scholars, poets, and officials across the Islamic world.
quotes: The biographer who omits the flaws of his subject has not written a biography — he has written flattery.
Every scholar who lived left something of himself in his words — literature is the resurrection of the dead.
The best memorial a man can leave is a book his students still read.

school: Shams al-Din Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Battuta (1304–1368), the greatest traveller of the pre-modern world. Over 29 years he covered approximately 120,000 kilometres — three times the distance of Marco Polo.

books.title: The Travels of Ibn Battuta (4 vols)
books.note: Complete Gibb English translation of the Rihla

books.title: The Travels of Ibn Battuta (Gibb translation)

quotes: Travelling leaves you speechless, then turns you into a storyteller.
I set out alone, having neither fellow-traveller in whose companionship I might find cheer, nor caravan whose party I might join — driven only by an overmastering impulse within me.
I have indeed — praise be to God — attained my desire in this world, which was to travel through the earth.
Travelling gives you home in a thousand strange places — and leaves you a stranger in your own land.

school: Sa’d al-Din al-Taftazani (1322–1390), a Central Asian scholar who produced the most widely studied textbooks of Islamic theology, logic, and rhetoric in the Ottoman and post-Mongol period. His commentaries on Maturidi and Ash’ari theology became the standard curriculum across the Muslim world for centuries.
quotes: The creed of the community is not a matter of individual opinion — it is what the scholars have transmitted and verified.
Words have meanings and meanings have consequences — the theologian must be precise with both.
To defend the religion with reason is itself an act of worship.

school: Taj al-Din al-Subki (1327–1370), one of the most eminent Shafi'i scholars of 14th-century Damascus. His Tabaqat al-Shafi'iyya al-Kubra is the definitive biographical dictionary of the Shafi'i school.
quotes: Consensus is a proof — but only when it is truly established, not merely claimed.
The scholar who clings to his own school against clear evidence has made his school his god.
Sound methodology requires: text first, then analogy, then consensus — in that order.

school: Maliki / Independent — Founded the philosophy of history and social science centuries before Hegel or Marx

books.title: Muqaddimah (English)
books.note: Introduction to History — first empirical philosophy of history

books.title: The Muqaddimah (English)
books.note: Foundational work on sociology, history and philosophy of history

quotes: The past resembles the future more than one drop of water resembles another.
Royal authority and large dynastic power are attained only through group feeling — the willingness of men to fight and die for one another.
The past resembles the future more than one drop of water resembles another.
Group feeling is the foundation of all dynastic power.

school: Hanbali School / Student of Ibn Qayyim — the final link in the great chain Ibn Taymiyya → Ibn Qayyim → Ibn Rajab; his biographical dictionary preserved the Hanbali intellectual tradition
quotes: The sign that God loves His servant is that He keeps the servant’s heart occupied with Him.
The happiest person is one who earns God’s pleasure even when it displeases people.
When calamity strikes, the believer knows it is a mercy in disguise.

school: A scholar of Arabic linguistics and literary theory active in the eleventh century, he authored two foundational works on Arabic rhetoric: Dala'il al-I'jaz (Proofs of Inimitability) and Asrar al-Balagha (Secrets of Eloquence). These treatises developed a sophisticated theory of meaning and composition that shaped Arabic literary criticism for centuries. His work on the inimitability of the Quran connected linguistics to theological discourse in innovative ways.
quotes: The science of rhetoric is the science of the heart of language.
Style is not decoration — it is the form that gives meaning its full weight.
Whoever masters language masters the most powerful instrument God gave to humanity.

school: Kamal al-Din al-Damiri (1341–1405) was an Egyptian Shafi'i scholar and naturalist best known for his monumental Hayat al-Hayawan al-Kubra (The Great Book of Animal Life), the most comprehensive zoological encyclopedia produced in the medieval Islamic world. Born in a village near Cairo, he studied hadith, jurisprudence, and literature before devoting years to compiling his zoological masterwork, which catalogues over 1,000 animals with entries covering natural history, Quranic references, prophetic traditions, Arabic poetry, medicinal uses, and dream interpretation. The work became a standard reference across the Islamic world for centuries. Al-Damiri also wrote on Shafi'i law and grammar, but it is the Hayat al-Hayawan that secured his enduring reputation as the greatest Islamic zoologist.
Akbarian / Ottoman — first Shaykh al-Islam; brought Ibn Arabi's philosophy into the Ottoman official curriculum

A fifteenth-century Egyptian historian and one of the most prolific writers of the Mamluk period, he authored over two hundred works on Egyptian history, topography, and administration. His Kitab al-Mawa'iz wa-l-I'tibar (known as al-Khitat) is an indispensable source for the historical topography of Cairo and Egypt. His economic writings analysed famines and currency crises with a sophistication remarkable for his era.
quotes: History is the memory of nations — destroy it and you destroy the nation's identity.
The ruins of the past speak louder than the words of the present.
When justice departs from a land, that land begins to die — slowly, then all at once.
I have walked the streets of Cairo and seen the ruins of what was great — let those who come after learn from them.

school: Chinese Muslim — eunuch admiral of the Ming Dynasty; his fleet dwarfed any contemporary European navy

Ottoman scholar (d. 1459) who was appointed the first chief judge (qadi) of Constantinople after its conquest by Mehmed II in 1453. He helped establish the educational and legal institutions of the newly Ottoman capital, founding madrasas and organising the judiciary. He had been the sultan’s personal tutor.

Ahmad al-Wansharisi (1430–1508), the leading Maliki jurist of late medieval North Africa. His al-Mi'yar al-Mu'rib is the largest surviving collection of North African and Andalusian Islamic legal opinions, running to twelve volumes.
quotes: The scholar who gives a ruling without full knowledge has betrayed his community.
Silence in matters of law is sometimes more pious than speaking.
Every era of oppression produces scholars of courage — God does not leave his people without guidance.
The Muslim who lives under non-Muslim rule and abandons the emigration has endangered his religion.
Law is not a wall — it is a path. The scholar’s task is to keep it open.
Every generation inherits the rulings of the past — and the duty to apply them with fresh wisdom.

school: A fifteenth-century Persian historian who lived in Herat under Timurid patronage, he authored Rawdat al-Safa (Garden of Purity), a universal history in Persian spanning from the creation to his own era. The work became one of the most widely read historical texts in the Persianate world and was continued after his death by his grandson Khwandamir. His accessible narrative style helped popularise historical knowledge among educated Persian-speaking audiences.
Shia philosophy — synthesised Sunni and Shia Sufism with Shia theology; bridge between Ibn Arabi and the Safavid school

Zenbilli Ali Cemali Efendi (1440–1526), Ottoman Shaykh al-Islam who served under three sultans for 25 years. Famous for lowering a basket (zenbil) from his window so people could submit questions anonymously.
Shadhiliyya-Zarruqiyya — reformed the Shadhili path; reconciled Sufism with strict Maliki law; wrote major commentary on al-Hikam

The great reconciler of Maliki jurisprudence and Shadhili mysticism; his Qawaid al-Tasawwuf (Principles of Sufism) established rational-legal criteria for evaluating Sufi practice, making it one of the first systematic methodologies for Sufi criticism; his commentaries on Al-Hikam of Ibn Ata Allah remain standard texts in Shadhili circles; founded the Zarruqiyya sub-branch in North Africa
Shafi'i / Egyptian — The last truly encyclopaedic Muslim scholar of the classical age; memorised 200,000 hadiths
quotes: A scholar's ink is more sacred than a martyr's blood.
I have authored over five hundred works — and I would have authored more were it not that life is short.
The scholar who ceases to learn has begun to die while still living.
I have authored over five hundred works — and would have authored more were it not that life is short.
The scholar who ceases to learn has begun to die while still living.

school: Kamal al-Din Behzad (c. 1450–1535), the greatest painter of the Persian miniature tradition, based at the Timurid court in Herat and later the Safavid court in Tabriz. His innovations in composition, colour, and psychological realism transformed Islamic painting. He is sometimes called the ‘Raphael of the East.’

Syrian female Sufi scholar and poet (1456–1517), the most prolific female author in Arabic literature before the modern era. A member of both the Qadiriyya and Shadhiliyya orders, she wrote over twenty books of poetry, mystical prose, and religious scholarship. She received ijazas from prominent male scholars of Damascus.

Chief architect of the Ottoman Empire (1489–1588) who designed over 300 structures including the Suleymaniye Mosque in Istanbul and the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne, considered his masterpiece. He transformed Ottoman architecture from imitation of the Hagia Sophia into an original artistic tradition. He served three sultans across fifty years.
Ebussuud Mehmed Efendi (1490–1574), Shaykh al-Islam under Suleiman the Magnificent for nearly 30 years, who achieved the harmonisation of Ottoman state law with Islamic jurisprudence.
Hanafi / Ottoman Theological School — criticized Sufi excesses; his Tariqa al-Muhammadiyya influenced Kadizadeli movement
Ash'ari school — his versified creed Jawharat al-Tawhid became the most studied theology text in the Islamic world for centuries
Maliki school of Timbuktu — the greatest scholar of West African Islam; captured and exiled to Marrakesh; wrote 40+ books
quotes: They took my books but they could not take what is in my mind.
Timbuktu was not built on gold — it was built on knowledge.
The scholar in chains is still freer than the ignorant man on a throne.

school: Indian Naqshbandi scholar (1564–1624) known as the Mujaddid (Renewer) of the Second Millennium. He opposed Emperor Akbar’s syncretic Din-i Ilahi and championed strict adherence to the Sharia within Sufi practice. His letters (Maktubat) are foundational texts of the Naqshbandiyya-Mujaddidiyya sub-order that spread across the Muslim world.
quotes: The purpose of the mystic path is not to lose yourself in God — it is to return to yourself, purified.
Sharia without tariqa is hypocrisy; tariqa without sharia is heresy.
The knower of God sees the world as a shadow that passes — and holds to what does not pass.
Do not be satisfied with states and visions — be satisfied only with God Himself.

school: Leading painter and calligrapher of the Safavid period (c. 1565–1635) who served under Shah Abbas I in Isfahan. He pioneered a new style combining single-figure portraits with flowing calligraphic lines, breaking from the Timurid miniature tradition. The Reza Abbasi Museum in Tehran is named in his honour.
Mulla Muhsin Fayd Kashani (1598–1680), a leading Safavid-era scholar, student and son-in-law of Mulla Sadra. He wrote the al-Wafi hadith commentary and al-Safi Quran commentary.
Rifa'iyya / Naqshbandiyya — chief religious authority of the Aceh Sultanate; combated heterodox Sufism; wrote first Malay encyclopedia
Ottoman scholarship — compiled Kashf al-Zunun, the most comprehensive bibliography of Islamic books ever written
quotes: The mind that does not travel in books cannot travel in the world.
History is the medicine of civilisation — nations that do not study their past will repeat its diseases.
The scholar who reads only one tradition has not yet begun to learn.

school: Ottoman — spent 40 years traveling the Islamic world; his Seyahatname is the most vivid and detailed portrait of 17th-century Islamic civilization
quotes: I have travelled forty years and found that every land has a secret that only the traveller discovers.
The city that does not welcome the stranger has forgotten that it was once built by strangers.
God put variety in the world so that the traveller would never run out of wonder.

school: Mughal prince and scholar (1615–1659), eldest son of Shah Jahan, who sought to find common ground between Islam and Hinduism. His Majma ul-Bahrayn (Meeting of Two Oceans) compared Sufi and Vedantic concepts. A devoted Qadiri Sufi, he translated the Upanishads into Persian. He was executed by his brother Aurangzeb after a succession war.
Shattariyya / Shadhiliyya — studied 19 years in Arabia; brought major Sufi orders and hadith sciences to Sumatra
Shadhiliyya — greatest Moroccan scholar of the 17th century; his letters challenging the Sultan on behalf of scholars are celebrated

Ottoman Sufi scholar (1653–1725) of the Celvetiyye order who wrote over 100 books, most notably the Ruh al-Bayan, a major mystical commentary on the Quran. Based in Bursa, he was one of the most prolific authors in Ottoman intellectual history.

Indian Islamic scholar and reformer (1703–1762) who sought to revive Islamic thought by returning to the Quran and hadith while embracing the insights of Sufism. Based in Delhi, he translated the Quran into Persian, bridged the Hanafi and Shafi’i schools, and influenced virtually every subsequent reform movement in South Asia.
quotes: The reform of any nation begins with the reform of its scholars.
The purpose of religion is not merely to govern actions — it is to transform the inner state from which all actions flow.
Whoever wishes to reform a people must first understand what corrupted them.
The reform of a community begins with the reform of its scholars.
Religion is not a collection of rituals — it is a transformation of the inner self.
The religion’s decline always begins with the scholars’ neglect of their duty.
Every nation that reforms itself must first reform its understanding of God’s word.
The Quran was revealed for all times — the scholar’s task is to make it speak to his age.
The religion’s decline always begins with the scholars’ neglect of their duty.
Every nation that reforms itself must first reform its understanding of God’s word.
The Quran was revealed for all times — the scholar’s task is to make it speak to his age.

school: Ottoman polymath (1703–1780) who combined Naqshbandi Sufi practice with encyclopedic learning. His Marifetname (Book of Knowledge) covers astronomy, anatomy, geography, ethics, and Sufi thought in a single volume — an Ottoman attempt to harmonise all knowledge under an Islamic framework.
Hanbali school — founded the Wahhabi/Salafi reform movement; allied with Muhammad ibn Saud; shaped modern Saudi Arabia
quotes: The purpose of my mission is to call people to the pure worship of God alone — nothing more.
The greatest act of worship is to know God through His own words and the words of His Prophet.

school: Qadiriyya Mukhtariyya — greatest West African Sufi scholar of his century; led a vast trans-Saharan network

Naqshbandiyya Mujaddidiyya — student of Mirza Mazhar Jan-e-Janan; his khanqah at Dargah Shah Ghulam Ali in Delhi became the spiritual capital of the Naqshbandiyya for a century; through Khalid al-Baghdadi he transformed the entire Ottoman-Arab Sufi world
Naqshbandiyya / Dehlawi School — Most authoritative Islamic scholar in 19th-century South Asia; his students founded the Deoband movement
quotes: The scholar who does not reform his own time has not understood his own knowledge.
Knowledge without action is a tree without fruit.

school: Sultan of Mysore — fiercest resistance to British colonialism in India; called himself 'citizen Tipu' after the French Revolution; died fighting the British at Srirangapatna

Fulani scholar and founder of the Sokoto Caliphate (1754–1817) in present-day Nigeria. Leading a jihad against syncretic practices, he established one of the largest states in 19th-century Africa. A prolific writer in Arabic, Fulfulde, and Hausa, he championed women’s education and wrote over 100 works on Islamic law and theology.
quotes: A government of injustice is more destructive to a country than sixty years of tyranny by enemies.
Teaching one man to read is more valuable than fighting a hundred battles.
The scholar who is silent in the face of injustice is a dumb devil.

school: Naqshbandiyya Khalidiyya — trained by Shah Ghulam Ali in Delhi, then spread the Naqshbandiyya into Ottoman Anatolia, the Balkans, and the Caucasus with unprecedented speed; today most Naqshbandis in the Middle East trace their lineage through him
Qadiriyya / West African Reform — second Sultan of Sokoto; son of Usman dan Fodio; authored over 60 works on Islamic governance
quotes: The pen of the scholar is the sword of the spirit.
Reform begins with the self — no community is reformed until its individuals are reformed.
Knowledge without character is a weapon in the hands of a child.

school: Ash'ari school / Al-Azhar — Shaykh al-Azhar; his commentaries on the Sanusiyya and Jawhara are the standard texts in traditional Islamic seminaries
Sokoto Caliphate — daughter of reformer Usman dan Fodio; founded the Yan Taru (The Associates), a network of female teachers travelling across the Caliphate to educate rural women
quotes: Education is not a privilege for the few — it is a duty upon every Muslim, man and woman.
The woman who teaches her sisters raises the whole community.
We do not fight ignorance with swords — we fight it with knowledge.

school: Tijaniyya — West African jihad leader and scholar; spread the Tijaniyya across West Africa; wrote major Sufi text

Sir Sayyid Ahmad Khan (1817–1898) was the most influential Muslim reformer and educationist in 19th-century British India. Born into a prominent Mughal-era family in Delhi, he witnessed the devastation of the 1857 uprising and concluded that Muslim advancement required modern Western education alongside Islamic values. He founded the Muhammadan Anglo-Oriental College at Aligarh in 1875, which later became Aligarh Muslim University — the institution that shaped generations of South Asian Muslim leaders. His Quran commentary used rationalist principles to reconcile scripture with natural science. He promoted Hindu-Muslim cooperation in his early career but later advocated for a distinct Muslim political identity. Known as Sir Syed, he remains one of the most consequential figures in the intellectual history of South Asian Islam.
quotes: Without education, Muslims will remain backward — and backwardness is not a religious virtue.
The Quran and modern science are not in conflict — they speak the same truth in different languages.
I want my community to raise its head with pride — and that pride must come from knowledge.

school: Pan-Islamist political activist and thinker (1838–1897) who travelled the Muslim world calling for unity, constitutional government, and the adoption of modern science. He influenced Muhammad Abduh in Egypt and political movements in Iran, India, and the Ottoman Empire. Often called the father of Islamic modernism and anti-colonial thought.
quotes: Unity is the secret of strength — division is the secret of weakness.
The nation that does not think for itself will be thought for by others.
Reform must begin with the mind — an enslaved mind cannot build a free nation.

school: Ottoman writer and political activist (1840–1888) who championed constitutionalism, liberty, and the homeland concept (vatan) during the Tanzimat reform era. His play Vatan yahut Silistre inspired patriotic fervour and led to his exile. He was a key figure in introducing Western political ideas into Ottoman-Islamic discourse.
quotes: Freedom is not a gift of rulers — it is a right that must be taken and defended.
The nation that does not read will be read by others — and the reading will not be kind.
Patriotism without justice is tribalism — justice without patriotism is abstraction.

school: Muhammad Abduh (1849–1905) was the Grand Mufti of Egypt and the most influential Islamic modernist thinker of the late 19th century. Born in a Nile Delta village, he studied at Al-Azhar University where he became a disciple of Jamal al-Din al-Afghani. Together they published the reformist journal al-Urwa al-Wuthqa in Paris. As Grand Mufti from 1899, Abduh issued fatwas that engaged with modern life — on banking, education, and women's rights — arguing that Islam's foundational texts were compatible with reason and progress. His Risalat al-Tawhid (Theology of Unity) reimagined Islamic theology for the modern era. He reformed Al-Azhar's curriculum to include modern sciences. His students — including Rashid Rida — carried his reformist legacy into the 20th century, making Abduh the single most important figure in the Islamic modernist movement.
quotes: I went to the West and saw Islam without Muslims — I came back to the East and saw Muslims without Islam.
The greatest obstacle to Islamic reform is the confusion between the religion and the culture.
Reason is not the enemy of revelation — it is its companion.

school: A Syrian intellectual and reformer of the late nineteenth century, he was one of the earliest Arab thinkers to articulate a vision of Arab nationalism and critique Ottoman despotism. His two major works, Tabai al-Istibdad (The Nature of Tyranny) and Umm al-Qura (The Mother of Cities), argued for constitutional governance and a renewed Arab cultural identity. He died in Cairo in 1902 under circumstances widely believed to have been poisoning by Ottoman agents.
Qadiriyya / Hanafi / Barelvi — Founded the Barelvi movement emphasising love of the Prophet; ~300 million followers worldwide
Chishti-Sabiri / Deobandi — Combined Deobandi scholarship with Chishti spirituality; his Bahishti Zewar shaped millions of lives

An Egyptian jurist and intellectual of the late nineteenth and early twentieth century, he is widely regarded as one of the founders of the Arab feminist movement. His two landmark books, Tahrir al-Mar'a (The Liberation of Women, 1899) and al-Mar'a al-Jadida (The New Woman, 1900), argued for women's education, legal reform, and an end to forced veiling, sparking intense public debate across the Arab world. His ideas drew on both Islamic reformist thought and European liberal philosophy.

Syrian-Egyptian Islamic reformer (1865–1935) who published the influential journal al-Manar for over thirty years. A student of Muhammad Abduh, he sought to reconcile Islamic tradition with modern science and governance. His ideas influenced both the Muslim Brotherhood and the Salafi reform movement, making him a bridge between classical and modern Islamic thought.
quotes: The Salaf were not rigid — they were principled. There is a difference.
The Muslim world will not revive until its scholars revive.
Reform the creed and the practice will follow — fix the root and the branches will be healthy.

school: Hanafi-Maturidi school — last Shaykh al-Islam of the Ottoman Empire; vigorous critic of secularism and Westernization
quotes: The scholar who abandons his principles under pressure has no principles — only positions.
Secularism is not neutral — it is a worldview that competes with revelation.
The defence of the Sharia is not conservatism — it is faithfulness.
The scholar who abandons his principles under pressure has no principles — only positions.
Secularism is not neutral — it is a worldview that competes with revelation.
The defence of the Sharia is not conservatism — it is faithfulness.

school: Nur movement — wrote Risale-i Nur collection from prison over 25 years; founded Turkey's most influential Islamic renewal
quotes: The Quran is an eternal miracle — it speaks to every age in the language of that age.
Despair is the greatest obstacle to progress — hope is the engine of every revival.
The one who knows God truly can face any hardship with a smile.
The Quran is an eternal miracle — it speaks to every age in the language of that age.
Despair is the greatest obstacle to progress — hope is the engine of every revival.
The one who knows God truly can face any hardship with a smile.
The Quran is a universe made of words — and the universe is a Quran made of things.
I am not afraid of death — I am afraid of a life not lived in God’s service.
Despair is the most dangerous disease of the age — hope is the remedy.

school: Maliki school — greatest modern North African Islamic scholar; his Maqasid al-Shariah reformed Islamic legal philosophy
quotes: The objectives of the Sharia are the key to understanding all its rulings.
A jurist who does not know the purpose of the law cannot apply it correctly.
The Sharia came to serve humanity — not to burden it.
The Sharia’s objectives are not additional to its rulings — they are the soul within them.
Every ruling that contradicts the objectives of the Sharia has been misread.
The jurist who does not serve the people has not served the law.

school: Bukharan intellectual and writer (1886–1938) who led the Jadidist movement for modernising Islamic education in Central Asia. He advocated for reformed madrasas teaching modern sciences alongside religious subjects. A novelist, playwright, and essayist, he was executed during Stalin’s purges.

An Egyptian Islamic scholar and Sharia judge, he provoked one of the most significant intellectual controversies of the twentieth-century Arab world with his 1925 book al-Islam wa-Usul al-Hukm (Islam and the Foundations of Governance). The work argued that the caliphate had no basis in the Quran or Prophetic tradition and that Islam prescribed no specific form of government, a position that challenged both traditional religious authority and contemporary political movements seeking to restore the caliphate. He was stripped of his scholarly credentials by Al-Azhar but his thesis profoundly influenced subsequent debates on Islam and secularism.

Algerian Islamic reformer (1889–1940) who led the Association of Algerian Muslim Scholars, reviving Arabic education and Islamic consciousness under French colonial rule. His journal al-Shihab championed a reformed Islam free of superstition and colonial influence. He is considered a founding father of modern Algerian national identity.
quotes: Islam is our religion, Arabic is our language, Algeria is our homeland.
The coloniser takes your land — do not let him take your mind.
Education in Arabic is not merely cultural preference — it is resistance.
Islam is our religion, Arabic is our language, Algeria is our homeland.
The coloniser takes your land — do not let him take your mind.
Education in Arabic is not merely cultural preference — it is resistance.

school: Hasan al-Banna (1906–1949), Egyptian teacher who founded the Muslim Brotherhood in 1928, the most influential Islamic social and political movement of the 20th century. He was assassinated by Egyptian government agents.
quotes: God is our objective, the Prophet is our leader, the Quran is our constitution, jihad is our way, and death for the sake of God is our highest aspiration.
The Muslim who does not work for the revival of his religion has not understood his religion.
Reform begins with the self — then the family, then the community, then the nation.
The Muslim who does not work for the revival of his religion has not understood his religion.
Reform begins with the self — then the family, then the community, then the nation.
Every Muslim is responsible for the state of his community — neutrality is not an option.

books.title: To What Do We Invite Humanity?

school: An Egyptian author, educator, and Islamist theorist who became the leading intellectual of the Muslim Brotherhood in the 1950s and 1960s. His prison writings, particularly Ma'alim fi al-Tariq (Milestones) and his multi-volume Quranic commentary Fi Zilal al-Quran (In the Shade of the Quran), articulated a radical critique of modern secular society and called for the establishment of an Islamic social order. He was executed by the Egyptian government in 1966, and his works have had a profound and contested influence on subsequent Islamist movements worldwide.
quotes: The Quran is not a book of theology alone — it is a programme for the transformation of reality.
Ignorance is not simply a lack of knowledge — it is the state of a society that has rejected God’s sovereignty.
The Muslim who is content with injustice has not understood his faith.

books.title: In the Shade of the Quran (selections)

school: Mehdi Bazargan (1907–1995) was an Iranian engineer, academic, and political leader who served as the first Prime Minister of Iran after the 1979 Islamic Revolution. Educated at the University of Paris in thermodynamics, he became a professor at Tehran University and pioneered an approach to Islamic thought that embraced modern science and democratic governance. He co-founded the Freedom Movement of Iran in 1961 and spent years in prison under the Shah. Appointed by Ayatollah Khomeini to lead the provisional government, he resigned after the Iran hostage crisis, opposing the direction of revolutionary politics. For the rest of his life he remained a vocal advocate for human rights and democratic Islam. His writings, including Thermodynamics of Man and The Boundary Between Religion and Social Affairs, attempted to reconcile Islamic faith with scientific modernity.
Islamic international law pioneer — wrote the first French translation of the Quran; expert on early Islamic state
Shadhiliyya-Alawiyya / Traditionalist school — author of the bestselling English biography of the Prophet

books.title: A Sufi Saint of the Twentieth Century: Shaikh Ahmad al-Alawi
books.note: Biography of the Algerian Sufi master — English

school: A twentieth-century South African Muslim missionary and orator of Indian Gujarati origin, he became internationally known for his public debates on comparative religion, particularly between Islam and Christianity. He founded the Islamic Propagation Centre International (IPCI) in Durban and authored numerous pamphlets and booklets on interfaith topics. His confrontational debating style and mastery of Biblical texts made him a polarising figure, admired by many Muslims for his da'wah efforts and criticised by others for his combative approach.

Pakistani-American scholar (1919–1988) who taught at the University of Chicago and developed a methodology for reinterpreting the Quran in light of its historical context and overarching ethical principles. His approach, known as the ‘double movement’ theory, influenced Islamic reformist thought worldwide. He was forced to leave Pakistan for his modernist views.
quotes: The Quran must be understood in its historical context — and then applied to our own.
Islamic reform requires the courage to distinguish the eternal from the historical.
The greatest danger to Islam is not secularism — it is intellectual stagnation.
The Quran must be understood in its historical context — and then applied to our own.
Islamic reform requires the courage to distinguish the eternal from the historical.
The greatest danger to Islam is not secularism — it is intellectual stagnation.

school: Palestinian-American scholar (1921–1986) who developed the concept of ‘Islamization of Knowledge’ — integrating modern disciplines with Islamic epistemology. He founded the International Institute of Islamic Thought and taught at Temple University. His comparative work on Abrahamic religions and his advocacy for Islamic intellectual revival influenced a generation of Muslim academics.
quotes: Tawhid is not only a theological statement — it is a comprehensive worldview.
The Islamisation of knowledge is not the rejection of modern learning — it is its reorientation.
Every discipline of knowledge must answer to the principle that there is no God but God.

school: African-American Muslim leader (1925–1965) who evolved from spokesman for the Nation of Islam to orthodox Sunni Islam after his 1964 Hajj, where the racial equality he witnessed transformed his worldview. His autobiography, co-written with Alex Haley, became one of the most influential books of the 20th century. He was assassinated in New York.

Egyptian-Qatari scholar (1926–2022) who was the most widely followed Sunni religious authority of the late 20th century. Based in Qatar, he hosted the al-Jazeera programme al-Sharia wal-Hayat and led the European Council for Fatwa and Research. He championed a centrist (wasatiyya) Islam and authored over 120 books.
quotes: The fiqh of priorities is more important than the fiqh of rulings.
Islam is ease — whoever makes it a burden has misunderstood it.
The scholar of our age must know both the text and the context.

school: Algerian-French scholar (1928–2010) who pioneered the application of modern critical theory to Islamic studies. Teaching at the Sorbonne, he argued for reading the Quran through the lens of contemporary hermeneutics, semiotics, and anthropology. His concept of the ‘unthought’ in Islamic thought challenged traditional scholarship and opened new fields of inquiry.
quotes: Islamic reason must be emancipated from its own orthodoxies before it can engage the modern world.
The sacred text becomes dangerous when it is treated as a closed system rather than an open horizon.
Every reading of a text is a construction — the honest scholar admits this.

school: A Syrian-American film director and producer, he is best known for directing The Message (1976), a major cinematic portrayal of the origins of Islam that was notable for not depicting the Prophet Muhammad on screen. He also directed Lion of the Desert (1981), depicting the Libyan resistance leader Omar Mukhtar's struggle against Italian colonial forces. His films brought Islamic and Arab history to international audiences and remain among the most widely viewed Islamic-themed productions.

Iranian sociologist and revolutionary thinker (1933–1977) who reinterpreted Shia Islam as a revolutionary ideology of liberation. His lectures at the Husayniyya-yi Irshad in Tehran electrified a generation of students. He synthesised Marxist sociology with Islamic thought, making him a key intellectual precursor of the Iranian Revolution.

books.title: Hajj: Reflections on Its Rituals
books.note: Philosophical and spiritual reading of the pilgrimage — English

quotes: Religion is not the opium of the people — it is the cry of the oppressed.
Waiting is itself a form of action — if the waiting is conscious and prepared.
The intellectual who does not serve his people has sold his mind to those who oppress them.

school: A Tunisian historian and intellectual of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries, he was one of the foremost modern scholars of early Islamic history writing in both French and Arabic. His trilogy on the life of the Prophet Muhammad and the founding of Islam applied rigorous historical-critical methodology to early Islamic sources, earning both acclaim and controversy. He also wrote influential works on Islamic civilisation, Arab identity, and the crisis of modern Arab culture.

Turkish Islamic scholar (1941–2024) who inspired the Hizmet (Service) movement, a global network of schools, media outlets, and interfaith dialogue initiatives. Emphasising education, science, and tolerance, his movement established over 1,000 schools worldwide. His relationship with the Turkish government deteriorated dramatically after 2013.
quotes: Love is the most essential element of every religion.
Education is the most effective way to change the world.
Tolerance is the most precious human virtue — without it, coexistence is impossible.

school: Nasr Hamid Abu Zayd (1943–2010) was an Egyptian Quranic scholar and one of the most important — and controversial — Muslim intellectuals of the late 20th century. Born in Tanta, he studied Arabic literature at Cairo University, where he developed a hermeneutic approach to the Quran that treated it as a literary text shaped by its historical and linguistic context while remaining divinely inspired. His works, including Mafhum al-Nass (The Concept of the Text) and Naqd al-Khitab al-Dini (Critique of Religious Discourse), provoked a firestorm. In 1995, an Egyptian court declared him an apostate and forcibly divorced him from his wife — a case that drew worldwide attention to the tensions between academic freedom and religious authority in the Muslim world. He and his wife moved to Leiden, Netherlands, where he taught until his death.
quotes: The text is a human reading of the divine — and human readings can be wrong.
The Quran speaks to all ages — but each age must learn to listen in its own way.
Criticism of religious interpretation is not criticism of religion — it is its protection.

school: Iranian philosopher of religion (b. 1945) who developed the theory of the ‘Contraction and Expansion of Religious Knowledge,’ arguing that while religion is divine, human understanding of it is historically conditioned and evolving. His ideas challenged clerical authority in Iran and made him one of the most influential Muslim intellectuals of the late 20th century.
quotes: Religion is divine — religious knowledge is human, and therefore fallible.
The Quran is not a constitution to be applied literally — it is a vision to be interpreted wisely.
Pluralism is not relativism — it is the recognition that truth is larger than any one tradition’s grasp of it.

school: A British-Pakistani scholar, writer, and public intellectual, he is known for his prolific work on Islamic futures, science and Islam, and postcolonial criticism. His books, including Desperately Seeking Paradise and Reading the Qur'an, blend personal memoir with intellectual inquiry into the challenges facing contemporary Muslims. He has been a prominent voice calling for reform and critical thinking within Muslim communities while challenging Western stereotypes of Islam.

The most influential Shafi'i jurist after the Imam himself, Ibn Surayj systematised and propagated the Shafi'i school from Baghdad. Known as 'the second al-Shafi'i', he reportedly wrote approximately 400 works and trained a generation of jurists who would define Islamic legal theory. Al-Suyuti named him as the Mujaddid of the 3rd Islamic century.

A towering hadith scholar and jurist who served as Chief Judge of Egypt, Ibn Daqiq al-'Id mastered both the Shafi'i and Maliki schools. His Ihkam al-Ahkam sharh Umdat al-Ahkam is considered one of the finest commentaries on legal hadith. Al-Suyuti named him as the Mujaddid of the 7th Islamic century.

The undisputed head of the Shafi'i school in Baghdad during the late 10th and early 11th centuries, Abu Hamid al-Isfarayini's legal circles attracted up to 700 jurists. His student al-Mawardi became one of the most celebrated political theorists in Islamic history. Al-Suyuti named him as a Mujaddid of the 4th Islamic century.

The Mufti of Nishapur and a leading Shafi'i jurist of the 10th century, al-Su'luki was recognised alongside al-Baqillani as a co-Mujaddid of the 4th Islamic century. He combined expertise in jurisprudence with deep knowledge of hadith sciences.

Muhammad Said Ramadan Al-Bouti (Arabic: مُحَمَّد سَعِيد رَمَضَان ٱلْبُوطِي, romanized: Muḥammad Saʿīd Ramaḍān al-Būṭī) (1929 – 21 March 2013) was a renowned Syrian Sunni Muslim scholar and author. He was served as professor and vice dean at the Damascus University, also serving as the imam of the Umayyad Mosque. Al-Bouti wrote more than sixty books on Islamic law and theology. He was a leading figure of Islamic neo-traditionalism which adhered to the four schools of thought in Sunni Islam and the orthodox Ash'arite creed. His works have been highly regarded to be a pivotal defense of Sunni Islam against opposing ideologies such as Secularism, Marxism, and Nationalism along with reformist movements of Wahhabism and Islamic Modernism. On 21 March 2013, al-Bouti was assassinated at the Al-Iman Mosque in Damascus. The circumstances around the event are still unclear.

Mollā Moḥammad bin Mortezā bin Maḥmūd Kāshānī, known as Mollā Moḥsen Fayz Kāshānī (Persian: ملا محسن فیض کاشانی) was an Iranian Akhbari Twelver Shi'i Muslim, mystic, poet, philosopher, and muhaddith (1598-1680 ᴄᴇ).

Al-Mu'ayyad fid-din Abu Nasr Hibat Allah b. Abi 'Imran Musa b. Da'ud ash-Shirazi (c. 1000 CE/390 AH – 1078 CE/470 AH) was an 11th-century Isma'ili scholar, philosopher-poet, preacher and theologian of Persian origin. He served the Fatimid Caliph-Imām al-Mustansir Billah as a Da'i in varying capacities, eventually attaining the highest rank of Bab al-Abwab "The Gate of Gates" and Da'i al-du'at "Chief Missionary" in the Fatimid Da‘wah. In his theological and philosophical writings he brought the Isma'ili spiritual heritage to its pinnacle.

Muhammad Ali Siddiqui (7 March 1938 – 9 January 2013) was a noted scholar of Urdu literature, educationist, literary critic and a newspaper columnist from Pakistan. He was also widely known by his pen name Ariel in Pakistan.

Muhammad Asad (born Leopold Weiss; 2 July 1900 – 20 February 1992) was an Austro-Hungarian Pakistani Muslim convert and polymath, born in modern day Ukraine. He worked as a journalist, traveler, writer, political theorist, and diplomat. Born to a Jewish family, Weiss had acquired a passing fluency in Hebrew and Aramaic by age 13, in addition to his native German and Polish languages. By his mid-twenties, he could read and write in English, French, Persian and Arabic. In Mandatory Palestine, Weiss engaged in arguments with Zionist leaders like Chaim Weizmann, voicing his reservations about some aspects of the Zionist Movement. After traveling across the Arab World as a journalist, he converted to Sunni Islam in 1926 and adopted the name "Muhammad Asad"—Asad being the Arabic rendition of his root name Leo (Lion). During his stay in Saudi Arabia, he spent time with Bedouins and enjoyed the close company of the state's founder, Ibn Saud. He also carried out a secret mission for Ibn Saud to trace the sources of funding for the Ikhwan Revolt.

books.title: The Road to Mecca
books.note: Autobiography of conversion and travel — English

school: Abu-al-Tayyab Shams-ul-Haq Azimabadi, (18 July 1857 - 21 March 1911), also known as Muhammad Shams-ul-Haq Azeemabadi, was a muhaddith, mujaddid and commentator of Hadith from British India. He is best known for writing the main commentary upon Sunan Abi Dawud.

Muhammad Talha Kandhlawi (28 May 1941 – 12 August 2019) was an Indian Islamic scholar and Sufi who served as the president of the advisory committee of Mazahir Uloom Jadeed and its secretary. He was the only son of Zakariyya Kandhlawi.

Muhammed Hamdi Yazır also known as Elmalılı Hamdi Yazır and Elmalılı (1878 – 27 May 1942) was a Turkish Maturidi theologian, logician, Qur'an translator, Qur'anic exegesis scholar, Islamic legal academic, philosopher and encyclopedist.

Musa Jarullah Bigiev (Tatar: Муса Ярулла Бигиев; 25 December 1873 – 28 October 1949) was a Tatar Muslim scholar, theologian, philosopher, and publicist. He was one of the leaders of the Jadid movement, an Islamic modernist movement in the Russian Empire. As a controversial reformist thinker, Bigi received the nickname "Luther of Islam" from the scholar, Ottoman Shaykh al-Islam Mustafa Sabri. After receiving his education in Kazan, Bukhara, Istanbul, and Cairo, he became a political activist for the Ittifaq, the political organisation of the Muslims of Russia. He also taught in Orenburg, wrote journalistic texts and translated classic works into Tatar. After emigrating from the Soviet Union, he travelled Europe and the Near and Far East while writing and publishing.

Nasir Subhani (Kurdish: ناسر سوبحانی, Persian: ناصر سبحانی; 14 October 1951 – 17 April 1990 or 19 March 1990) was a Kurdish Sunni scholar from Iranian Kurdistan. He was born in the village of Durisan, near Paveh, Sanandaj in Iran. He was outspoken against the current regime of Iran. He was executed by Iranian authorities on or before 17 April 1990 having been detained earlier.

Nasrollah Pourjavady is an Iranian philosopher, Sufi scholar and a professor of philosophy at the University of Tehran in Tehran, Iran. He is the founder and former head of the Iran University Press and a permanent member of the Academy of Persian Language and Literature.

Rifa'a Rafi' at-Tahtawi (Arabic: رفاعة رافع الطهطاوي, romanized: Rifāʿa Rāfiʿ aṭ-Ṭahṭāwī; 1801–1873) was an Egyptian writer, teacher, translator, Egyptologist, and intellectual of the Nahda (the Arab renaissance). One of the first Egyptian travellers to France in the nineteenth century, Tahtawi published in 1834 a detailed account of his 5-year-long stay in France, Takhlis al-ibriz fi talkhis Bariz ('The Extrication of Gold in Summarizing Paris'), and from then on became one of the first Egyptian scholars to write about Western culture in an attempt to bring about a reconciliation and an understanding between Islamic and Christian civilizations. In 1835 he founded a School of Languages in Cairo, and he was influential in the development of science, law, literature, and Egyptology in 19th-century Egypt. His works influenced those of many later scholars such as Muhammad Abduh.
