Sayf al-Din al-Amidi was a leading Ash'ari theologian and Shafi'i jurist. His magnum opus 'Abkar al-Afkar' is one of the most sophisticated works of Islamic systematic theology. He also authored 'al-Ihkam fi Usul al-Ahkam,' a foundational text in Islamic legal theory that was widely studied alongside al-Razi's works.

Shibli Nomani was a leading Indian Islamic scholar, historian, and literary critic. He founded the Shibli Academy and authored major biographical works including 'Sirat al-Nabi' (Life of the Prophet) and 'Al-Mamun,' a study of the Abbasid caliph. His historical methodology combined traditional Islamic scholarship with modern critical approaches.

Syed Abul Ala Maududi was one of the most influential Islamic thinkers of the twentieth century. He founded Jamaat-e-Islami in 1941 and developed a comprehensive vision of Islamic governance that shaped political Islam globally. His extensive Quran commentary 'Tafhim al-Quran' and works on Islamic law and politics have been translated into dozens of languages.

Sir Syed Ahmed Khan was the most influential Muslim reformer of nineteenth-century India. He founded the Muhammadan Anglo-Oriental College at Aligarh (later Aligarh Muslim University), pioneering modern education for Indian Muslims. His rationalist approach to Quranic interpretation and his advocacy for engagement with Western science generated lasting debate within Indian Islam.

Maulana Wahiduddin Khan was a prominent Indian Islamic scholar, writer, and peace activist. He authored over 200 books on Islam, peace, and interfaith dialogue and founded the Centre for Peace and Spirituality. His approach emphasised non-violence, coexistence, and a spiritual reading of Islam that earned him wide recognition across religious boundaries.

Adud al-Din al-Iji was one of the most important Ash'ari theologians of the medieval period. His 'al-Mawaqif fi Ilm al-Kalam' became the standard advanced textbook in Islamic systematic theology, studied with its commentary by al-Jurjani across the Islamic world for centuries. He also served as a judge under the Ilkhanid dynasty in Iran.

Abu al-Aswad al-Du'ali is traditionally credited as the founder of Arabic grammar (nahw). According to Islamic tradition, he developed the first system of diacritical marks for the Quran at the behest of Ali ibn Abi Talib to prevent misreadings of the sacred text. His pioneering work on Arabic linguistic rules laid the foundation for the Basran school of grammar.

Abu'l-Fazl ibn Mubarak was the most brilliant intellectual at Emperor Akbar's court. He authored the 'Akbarnama,' the official chronicle of Akbar's reign, and its companion volume 'Ain-i-Akbari,' an extraordinary administrative gazetteer of the Mughal Empire. He was also the chief architect of Akbar's policy of sulh-i kul (universal peace) and interreligious dialogue.

Ahmad Faris al-Shidyaq was a Lebanese-born polymath who revolutionised Arabic prose style. His masterwork 'al-Saq ala al-Saq' is considered one of the first modern Arabic novels. He founded the influential newspaper 'al-Jawa'ib' in Istanbul, translated the Bible into Arabic, and after converting to Islam became a leading figure in Ottoman intellectual life. He is regarded as a father of the Arabic Nahda.

Al-Khalil ibn Ahmad al-Farahidi was one of the greatest linguists of the Islamic world. He invented the science of Arabic prosody (arud), systematising the meters of Arabic poetry, and compiled 'Kitab al-Ayn,' the first comprehensive dictionary of the Arabic language. His student Sibawayhi would build on his work to produce the foundational grammar of Arabic.

Shihab al-Din al-Nuwayri was an Egyptian scholar and bureaucrat who authored 'Nihayat al-Arab fi Funun al-Adab' (The Ultimate Ambition in the Arts of Erudition), a massive encyclopaedia in 31 volumes covering natural history, geography, literature, and history. It is one of the most comprehensive works of medieval Arabic prose and an invaluable source on Mamluk-era knowledge.

Ata-Malik Juvayni was a Persian historian and administrator who served the Mongol Ilkhanids. His 'Tarikh-i Jahan-Gusha' (History of the World-Conqueror) is the most important contemporary account of the Mongol conquests and the rise of the Mongol Empire. He later served as governor of Baghdad and his historical work remains indispensable for understanding the thirteenth-century Islamic world.

Hamdallah Mustawfi was a Persian historian, geographer, and poet who served the Ilkhanid administration in Qazvin. His 'Nuzhat al-Qulub' is a major geographical work on Iran and his 'Tarikh-i Guzida' is a valuable universal history. His detailed descriptions of Persian cities and provinces are indispensable sources for the study of medieval Iran.

Ibn Duraid was a major Arab lexicographer, grammarian, and poet. His 'Kitab al-Jamhara' is one of the great early Arabic dictionaries, and his 'al-Maqsura' is a celebrated poem on the Arabic language. He contributed significantly to the preservation and systematisation of Arabic vocabulary and genealogical knowledge.

Kemalpaşazade (Ibn Kemal) was one of the most distinguished Ottoman scholars, serving as Shaykh al-Islam under Suleiman the Magnificent. He authored over 200 works in Arabic, Persian, and Turkish on subjects ranging from Quranic exegesis and jurisprudence to history and literature. His 'Tevarih-i Al-i Osman' is an important Ottoman dynastic history.

Sharif al-Murtada was a leading Shia theologian, jurist, and literary scholar in Abbasid Baghdad. The elder brother of Sharif al-Radi, he developed important positions in Shia kalam and was a proponent of the rationalist Mu'tazili approach within Shia thought. He also served as naqib al-ashraf and authored important works on Arabic poetry and rhetoric.

Usama ibn Munqidh was a Syrian Arab knight, poet, and diplomat whose memoir 'Kitab al-I'tibar' (Book of Contemplation) is one of the most vivid firsthand accounts of the Crusades from the Muslim perspective. His observations of Frankish customs and warfare, interspersed with hunting anecdotes and personal reflections, make his book a unique document of twelfth-century life.

Al-Qadi al-Nu'man was the most important jurist of the Fatimid Caliphate and the founder of Ismaili jurisprudence. His 'Da'a'im al-Islam' (Pillars of Islam) is the foundational legal code of Ismaili Shia law, still followed by the Dawoodi Bohra community today. He also authored major historical works on the Fatimid dynasty and North African Islamic history.

Ibrahim Muteferrika was a Hungarian-born Ottoman polymath who established the first printing press with moveable Arabic type in the Muslim world in 1729. He printed maps, scientific works, and histories that introduced European geographical and military knowledge to the Ottoman reading public. His essay 'Usul al-Hikam' analysed the reasons for Ottoman military decline.

Abd al-Hayy al-Lucknawi was a remarkably prolific Indian Islamic scholar who died at only 38 but left behind over 100 works. He was a leading authority on Hanafi jurisprudence and hadith criticism, and his commentaries and bibliographical works became standard references. His mastery of Arabic Islamic sciences was considered extraordinary for a scholar based in colonial India.

Abd al-Razzaq Samarqandi was a Persian historian and diplomat who served the Timurid court at Herat. His chronicle 'Matla al-Sa'dayn' covers Timurid history and includes a valuable account of his embassy to the Hindu kingdom of Vijayanagara in India. His work is an important source on fifteenth-century Central Asian and Indian history.

Abdolkarim Haeri Yazdi was an Iranian Shia marja who founded the modern hawza (seminary) of Qom in 1922, transforming it into the premier centre of Shia learning. His decision to establish the seminary at Qom shifted the centre of gravity of Shia scholarship from Najaf to Iran and laid the institutional foundation for the later political role of the Qom clergy.

Abd al-Rahman al-Jabarti was the last great chronicler of pre-modern Egypt. His 'Aja'ib al-Athar' (Marvellous Compositions of Biographies and Events) provides the most detailed Egyptian eyewitness account of Napoleon's 1798 invasion and the subsequent rise of Muhammad Ali. His work marks the transition from medieval Islamic historiography to the modern era.

Abu Bakr ibn al-Arabi was a prominent Andalusian Maliki scholar who studied under al-Ghazali in Baghdad and brought his master's ideas to the Islamic West. His Quran commentary 'Ahkam al-Quran' is a major work of legal exegesis, and his 'al-Awasim min al-Qawasim' is a noted work defending the early Muslim community. He served as qadi of Seville.

Abu Hafs Umar al-Nasafi was a Central Asian Hanafi jurist and theologian. He authored over 100 works, but is best known for his 'Aqida Nasafiyya,' a concise creed that became one of the most widely taught theological texts in Sunni madrasas. His works on jurisprudence, Quran exegesis, and biography were standard references in the Hanafi tradition.

Abul Hasan Ali Hasani Nadwi was one of the most influential Indian Islamic scholars of the twentieth century. His book 'Madha Khasira al-Alam bi Inhitat al-Muslimin' (What the World Lost by the Decline of Muslims) was acclaimed across the Arab world and established him as a major voice in Islamic intellectual discourse. He served as rector of Nadwat al-Ulama and was a member of numerous Islamic academies.

Ahmad Amin was a leading Egyptian historian and man of letters. His trilogy on Islamic civilisation — 'Fajr al-Islam,' 'Duha al-Islam,' and 'Zuhr al-Islam' — traces the intellectual and cultural history of early Islam and remains a standard reference in Arabic. He also authored an influential autobiography 'Hayati' and edited the literary journal 'al-Thaqafa.'

Ahmad ibn Fadlan was a tenth-century Abbasid diplomat sent on a mission to the Volga Bulgars. His travel account, the 'Risala,' contains the most detailed contemporary description of the Rus Vikings, including their ship burial rituals, and is an invaluable ethnographic source on the peoples of the Eurasian steppe. His account inspired Michael Crichton's novel 'Eaters of the Dead.'

Ahmad ibn Arabshah was an Arab historian best known for his biography of Timur (Tamerlane), 'Aja'ib al-Maqdur fi Naba'i Timur.' Captured as a child during Timur's sack of Damascus, he spent years in Central Asia before returning to the Arab world. His hostile but vivid account of Timur's conquests is one of the most important primary sources on the Timurid period.

Ashraf Ali Thanwi was one of the most influential scholars of the Deobandi tradition in India. His 'Bahishti Zewar' (Heavenly Ornaments), a guide to Islamic practice for women, became the most widely read Urdu religious text in South Asia. A prolific author of over 800 works, he combined expertise in jurisprudence with deep Sufi learning in the Chishti tradition.

Amin Ahsan Islahi was a Pakistani Quranic scholar whose nine-volume commentary 'Tadabbur-i-Quran' is one of the most significant modern works of Urdu Quran exegesis. He developed the hermeneutical approach of his teacher Hamiduddin Farahi, emphasising the coherent thematic structure (nazm) of the Quran. His methodology influenced a generation of Pakistani and Indian Islamic scholars.

Asad ibn al-Furat was a Maliki jurist and military commander from Ifriqiya (modern Tunisia). He studied under Imam Malik in Medina and compiled the 'Asadiyya,' an important early text of Maliki jurisprudence. He later led the Aghlabid naval expedition that began the Muslim conquest of Sicily in 827, dying during the siege of Syracuse.

Azad Bilgrami was an Indian polymath celebrated for his mastery of Arabic, Persian, and Urdu literary sciences. He authored over 100 works and was recognised by scholars in Arabia as one of the foremost Arabic stylists of his age. His biographical dictionaries of Indian poets and scholars are important sources on the intellectual history of Mughal India.

Azyumardi Azra was Indonesia's most internationally recognised Islamic scholar. His groundbreaking doctoral research traced the scholarly networks connecting Southeast Asian Muslims with the Middle East in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. He served as rector of UIN Syarif Hidayatullah Jakarta and was a leading voice for moderate Islam in the world's largest Muslim-majority nation.

Baha al-Din ibn Shaddad was a Kurdish scholar and qadi who served as Saladin's personal judge and confidant. His biography of Saladin, 'al-Nawadir al-Sultaniyya,' is one of the most important primary sources on the great Kurdish-Ayyubid sultan and the Third Crusade. He later served as chief qadi of Aleppo under Saladin's successors.

Ali Abdel Raziq was an Egyptian Islamic scholar and Azhari judge whose 1925 book 'al-Islam wa Usul al-Hukm' (Islam and the Foundations of Governance) argued that Islam does not prescribe a specific political system and that the caliphate is not a religious obligation. The book provoked enormous controversy, led to his expulsion from al-Azhar, and remains one of the most debated works in modern Islamic political thought.

Abd al-Razzaq al-Sanhuri was an Egyptian jurist who is considered the father of modern Arab civil law. He drafted the civil codes of Egypt, Iraq, Libya, Kuwait, and several other Arab states, synthesising principles from Islamic jurisprudence, French civil law, and comparative legal traditions. His monumental work 'al-Wasit' on Egyptian civil law runs to over ten volumes.

Abdelwahab Meddeb was a Tunisian-French writer, poet, and intellectual. His works, including 'The Malady of Islam,' offered a sophisticated critique of Islamic fundamentalism while championing the rich literary and mystical heritage of Islamic civilisation. He hosted the France Culture radio programme 'Cultures d'Islam' and was a professor of comparative literature at Paris X University.

Ahmad ibn Abi Jum'ah was an Algerian Maliki jurist known for issuing the famous 'Oran fatwa' of 1504, which permitted Muslims forcibly converted to Christianity in Spain to practice their faith secretly. The fatwa allowed crypto-Muslims (Moriscos) to eat pork, drink wine, and outwardly conform to Christianity under duress, while maintaining Islam internally. It is a landmark document in Islamic jurisprudence on religious coercion.

Asikpasazade was one of the earliest Ottoman historians whose chronicle of the Ottoman dynasty covers its origins through the reign of Bayezid II. A descendant of the Sufi poet Asik Pasha, he participated in Ottoman campaigns and wrote from both personal experience and oral tradition. His chronicle is an essential primary source on early Ottoman history and the role of dervish orders in Ottoman expansion.

Chiragh Ali was an Indian Muslim scholar and reformist thinker associated with the Aligarh movement of Sir Syed Ahmad Khan. He argued for the reform of Islamic law and challenged traditional interpretations of jihad, advocating a contextual reading of Quranic legislation. His English-language works on Islamic law and warfare addressed Western audiences and contributed to the modernist reform discourse.

Imam Musa al-Sadr was an Iranian-born Shia cleric who transformed the political and social position of the Shia community in Lebanon. He founded the Movement of the Deprived and its militia Amal, advocating for Shia rights within Lebanon's confessional system. His mysterious disappearance during a visit to Libya in 1978 made him a martyr figure in Lebanese Shia consciousness.

Abdulbaki Golpinarli was a Turkish scholar who was the foremost twentieth-century authority on Sufism, particularly the Mevlevi order. He edited and translated the works of Rumi, Yunus Emre, and other major Sufi poets into modern Turkish. His scholarly works on the history of Turkish Sufi orders and his critical editions of classical texts remain indispensable references.

Abu Ishaq al-Shatibi was a Granadan Maliki jurist whose 'al-Muwafaqat' is considered the foundational text of maqasid al-sharia (objectives of Islamic law). He argued that Islamic law should be understood in terms of its overarching purposes — the protection of religion, life, intellect, lineage, and property. His work has been enormously influential in modern Islamic legal reform.
quotes: The objectives of the Sharia are the preservation of religion, life, intellect, lineage, and wealth.
Any legal ruling that contradicts the purpose of the law is a deviation from the law itself.

school: Fatema Mernissi was a Moroccan sociologist and one of the founders of Islamic feminism. Her works 'Beyond the Veil' and 'The Veil and the Male Elite' challenged patriarchal interpretations of Islamic texts by returning to the original sources. She argued that the Prophet Muhammad's practice was far more egalitarian than later Muslim societies acknowledged.

Fuat Sezgin was a Turkish historian of science whose monumental 'Geschichte des arabischen Schrifttums' (History of Arabic-Islamic Scientific Literature) in seventeen volumes is the most comprehensive bibliography of Islamic science ever compiled. He documented the contributions of Muslim scientists to global civilisation and founded the Institute for the History of Arab-Islamic Science in Frankfurt.

Charles Le Gai Eaton was a British diplomat and writer who converted to Islam and became one of the most eloquent English-language interpreters of the faith. His 'Islam and the Destiny of Man' is considered a classic introduction to Islam for Western readers. He served as a consultant to the Islamic Cultural Centre in London and his writings blended Islamic tradition with the Perennialist philosophical perspective.

Taha Hussein was the most influential Arab literary figure of the twentieth century. Blind from childhood, he became dean of the Faculty of Arts at Cairo University and Egypt's Minister of Education. His autobiography 'al-Ayyam' (The Days) is a masterpiece of modern Arabic prose, and his critical works on pre-Islamic poetry provoked a foundational debate about literary heritage and modernism.

Halil Inalcik was the most authoritative historian of the Ottoman Empire in the twentieth century. His 'The Ottoman Empire: The Classical Age 1300-1600' and his editorship of the Cambridge History of Turkey set the standard for Ottoman studies globally. He taught at the University of Ankara and the University of Chicago and trained generations of Ottoman historians.

Hichem Djait was a Tunisian historian and one of the leading scholars of early Islamic history in the Arab world. His multi-volume study of the life of Prophet Muhammad applied modern historical methodology to the Islamic sources. He also authored influential works on the cultural identity of the Arab-Islamic world and served as professor at the University of Tunis.

Hisham ibn al-Kalbi was the foremost Arab genealogist and antiquarian of the early Abbasid period. His 'Kitab al-Asnam' (Book of Idols) is the most detailed surviving account of pre-Islamic Arabian religion and idol worship. His genealogical works systematised the tribal lineages of the Arabs and became foundational references for later Islamic historians.

Ibn al-Qalanisi was a Damascene historian and civic leader whose chronicle 'Dhayl Tarikh Dimashq' (Continuation of the History of Damascus) is the earliest Arabic chronicle of the Crusades. His eyewitness account of the First Crusade and its aftermath, written from the perspective of a Damascene notable, is an indispensable counterpart to the Latin Crusader sources.

Sibt ibn al-Jawzi was a historian and preacher in Damascus, grandson of the famous Hanbali scholar Ibn al-Jawzi of Baghdad. He compiled 'Mir'at al-Zaman' (Mirror of Time), a massive universal chronicle that is an important source on the Ayyubid period and the Mongol invasions. He converted from the Hanbali to the Hanafi school and became a popular preacher in Damascus.

Lubna of Cordoba was a woman of Iberian origin who rose to become the personal secretary and librarian of the Umayyad Caliph al-Hakam II. She was skilled in mathematics and the copying of manuscripts, and played a central role in building the great caliphal library of Cordoba, reputed to contain 400,000 volumes. She is one of the most notable women of learning in al-Andalus.

Ali ibn al-Husayn al-Mas'udi was an Arab historian and geographer known as 'the Herodotus of the Arabs.' His 'Muruj al-Dhahab' (Meadows of Gold) combines history, geography, ethnography, and natural science in a vivid narrative that covers the known world from China to the Franks. He travelled extensively and his encyclopaedic approach to history made him one of the most original minds of the Islamic golden age.

school: Ahmad ibn Abi Ya'qub al-Ya'qubi was an early Islamic historian and geographer. His universal history 'Tarikh al-Ya'qubi' is one of the earliest surviving works of Islamic historiography, covering world history from creation to the Abbasid period. His geographical work 'Kitab al-Buldan' is a valuable source on the cities and provinces of the ninth-century Islamic world.

Shihab al-Din al-Umari was a Damascene encyclopedist who served in the Mamluk chancery. His monumental 'Masalik al-Absar fi Mamalik al-Amsar' (Pathways of Vision in the Kingdoms of the Great Cities) is a vast encyclopaedic work describing the states and peoples of the known world. His account of Mansa Musa's pilgrimage is the most detailed source on the wealth of the Mali Empire.
